reply_text:
@ponv6 I might be overthinking it, but seeing some of the reactions on Twitter made me feel a bit uneasy...

That’s why I really appreciate people like Junichi Okada, who are actively trying to bring Japanese works to the global stage. I'm genuinely happy when people from other countries create works based on Japanese themes. And since those works are made by non-Japanese creators, I don’t mind a few inaccuracies—as long as they’re not seriously disrespectful toward the culture. After all, Japanese creators also make mistakes when depicting foreign cultures.

Still, it’s always better when a culture is represented accurately. So I’d be really glad if more works about Japanese culture, created by Japanese people, could be seen and appreciated around the world.

As a Japanese person myself, I take pride in our culture. I  love how Japanese culture has evolved uniquely from elements that originally came from China. I know I can’t do much myself, but I truly believe in the power of creators!

reply_text:
But Squid game not inspired by any show either.. 

Squid Game was inspired by creator Hwang Dong-hyuk's experiences with financial struggles and the widening class disparity and intense competition in South Korean society, along with memories of playing traditional children's games. While the show isn't based on a single real event, the themes reflect the harsh realities of economic hardship, debt crises, and social inequality in South Korea. 

Creator's Personal Experience

Economic Hardship:

Hwang Dong-hyuk conceived the idea for the show due to his own personal economic difficulties and his struggles to make ends meet. 

Childhood Games:

The traditional children's games featured in the show, such as ddakji and gonggi, were familiar to him from his own childhood. 

Social and Economic Issues

Class Disparity:

A major inspiration was the significant class gap and social inequality present in South Korea. 

Capitalism and Debt:

The show explores themes of cut-throat competition and the pressures of capitalism, which are reflected in South Korea's real-life debt crisis. 

Traditional Korean Games 

Historical Context:

Games like ddakji and gonggi date back centuries, while others, like the marble shooting and dalgona candy challenge, became popular in the mid-20th century, especially after the Korean War.

Childhood in Hard Times:

These games were popular during economic difficulties, requiring minimal materials and offering a contrast to today's digital entertainment.

Social Connection:

Unlike modern digital games, these traditional games fostered social interaction, creativity, and strategic thinking.
